title: Replacing disposable gift wrapping with reusable and recyclable alternatives
subtitle: Practical options for wrapping gifts without single-use plastic-coated paper, and how to make them work at scale
excerpt: Around 108 million rolls of wrapping paper are thrown away in the UK each year, most of it plastic-coated and therefore not recyclable in kerbside collections. Fabric wrapping, paper that passes the scrunch test, brown kraft paper, and boxes that become the packaging all reduce or eliminate this waste without requiring guests to return anything or change their behaviour.
sourceNotes: WRAP UK 'Festive waste' evidence review (2022). Recycle Now wrapping paper guidance. Furoshiki UK fabric wrapping practitioners. Paper Industry Technical Association guidance on coated paper recyclability.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Japanese wrapping technique using a square of fabric to wrap objects of almost any shape. The cloth can be unwrapped and reused or kept as part of the gift. Traditional techniques use knots to secure the cloth; no tape or ribbon is required.

A quick field test for recyclability: scrunch a piece of paper in your hand. If it holds its scrunched shape, it is paper-fibre and likely recyclable in standard kerbside paper collections. If it springs back, it contains plastic coating or laminate and should go in general waste.

content.text: Conventional glossy wrapping paper usually fails the

because the finish that gives it its appearance is a plastic laminate. It cannot go in paper recycling and ends up in landfill or incineration. The alternatives below do not require anyone else to change what they do with the wrapping after they receive it, except for fabric wrapping where the cloth is kept or returned.

Brown kraft paper: passes the scrunch test, recyclable, available in large rolls from online suppliers for roughly 5 to 8 pounds per 50 metres. Decorate with rubber stamps, wax seals, dried sprig of foliage, or printed twine. A 50-metre roll wraps approximately 30 to 50 average gifts depending on size.
Newspaper and magazine pages: free, available now, and recyclable. Use string rather than tape to keep it all recyclable.
Paper bags retained from shopping: reuse before recycling. A handled paper bag with ribbon handles is sufficient for many gift sizes without any wrapping.

technique uses a square cloth to wrap almost any shape without tape. A 50 cm square wraps a wine bottle; a 70 cm square wraps a shoebox-sized gift. The receiver keeps the cloth as part of the gift or returns it at the next occasion. Old scarves, tea towels, or fabric remnants cut into squares work without hemming for most uses.
Tins and jars: a sealed tin or jar filled with homemade biscuits, jam, or preserved goods is its own container. The recipient keeps the container. No wrapping required; tie a label on the lid.
Baskets and boxes: a wicker basket or wooden crate filled with gifts becomes a reusable storage item. These can be found second-hand at charity shops for 1 to 3 pounds each.
Canvas bags: a reusable bag containing the gift is itself a useful item. No wrapping needed; a card tucked inside is sufficient.
Glitter is a microplastic. It cannot be recycled. It ruins paper recycling batches it is mixed with.

Plastic tape on kraft paper: a small piece of plastic tape makes otherwise-recyclable paper difficult to process cleanly. Use paper tape or kraft tape (both cellulose-based and recyclable with the paper).
Tissue paper: most coloured tissue is dyed with pigment that cannot be removed. It is not accepted in most household collections. It adds nothing structurally, so leave it out.
